does: This paper introduces Oracle Poisoning, a novel attack class where adversaries corrupt knowledge graphs that AI agents query during tool-use, leading agents to reach incorrect conclusions through otherwise sound reasoning applied to poisoned data. The authors evaluate this attack empirically against a production 42-million-node code knowledge graph, demonstrating six attack scenarios across nine models from three major providers using real SDK tool-use protocols. They find that all tested models accept poisoned data with 100% trust under moderate attacker sophistication in directed query scenarios (269 of 270 trials), accepting fabricated security claims. Trust drops to 3-55% under open-ended prompts, indicating that query framing significantly influences vulnerability. The work provides the first empirical demonstration of knowledge graph poisoning at production scale against agentic systems, distinctly different from prior work on embedding poisoning attacks.
connects: This work extends research on AI safety vulnerabilities and adversarial attacks on language models by shifting focus from instruction-level manipulation to data-level corruption. It builds on prior work examining prompt injection and jailbreaking techniques, but targets the reasoning pipeline rather than model instructions. The paper connects to broader research on knowledge graph integrity, tool-use vulnerabilities in AI agents, and how models handle structured external information sources during reasoning. It relates to adversarial robustness literature that examines how models behave when given malicious inputs, and parallels work on data poisoning in machine learning contexts. The distinction from CTI embedding poisoning suggests grounding in cybersecurity threat intelligence literature while introducing a novel surface of attack specific to agentic systems relying on structured data.
enables: This research opens investigation into defenses against data poisoning in agentic architectures, including verification mechanisms for external tool outputs, source credibility assessment during reasoning, and anomaly detection for suspicious knowledge graph queries. It enables future work on building robust tool-use protocols that validate or triangulate results across multiple knowledge sources. The stark difference in trust levels between directed and open-ended prompts points toward exploring how prompt engineering and query design affect data verification. The findings enable development of better agent architectures that maintain epistemic humility about external data sources, implement cross-validation of critical claims, and make explicit the reliability assumptions agents make. This work also motivates deeper study of how different model architectures and training procedures affect susceptibility to data poisoning, and informs design of knowledge graph access controls and integrity checking for production AI systems.
